frontier_title: Position: Enough of Scaling LLMs! Lets Focus on Downscaling
trajectory_ascii:
Position: Enough of Scaling LLMs! L... (2025)
├── ActiveLLM: Large Language Model-bas... (2024)  (background)
├── Bridging the Gap between Different ... (2024)  (background)
├── D-CPT Law: Domain-specific Continua... (2024)  (background)
├── Ensemble Learning for Heterogeneous... (2024)  (background)
├── Merge, Ensemble, and Cooperate! A S... (2024)  (background)
├── Scaling Laws for Predicting Downstr... (2024)  (background)
├── Small Language Models: Survey, Meas... (2024)  (background)
└── Scaling Laws with Vocabulary: Large... (2024)  (background)
trajectory_description: The research tree traces a shift from 2024 studies on scaling laws, ensemble strategies, vocabulary optimization, domain-specific pre-training, and small language models to a 2025 position paper that challenges the dominant scaling paradigm. These diverse background investigations—covering active learning, heterogeneous model collaboration, downstream performance prediction, and model merging—converge on the insight that scaling up alone may be inefficient, leading the frontier work to advocate for downscaling as a more sustainable and effective direction. Overall, the narrative reflects a maturing field that moves from exploring how to make large models better through scaling and ensembling to questioning the necessity of size itself, emphasizing efficiency, selective training, and strategic downsizing.

e0ed93eaee6ebd6635b6411d6da6c641975e929e.title: Position: Enough of Scaling LLMs! Lets Focus on Downscaling

entities.name: Neural scaling laws
entities.description: Relationship between model/dataset size and performance improvements in LLMs.

entities.name: Downscaling
entities.description: Paradigm shift to maintain performance while reducing resource demands.

entities.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
entities.description: Neural network models trained on large text corpora for language tasks.

idea.background: Current LLM development heavily relies on neural scaling laws, which show performance gains from increasing model and dataset size. However, this approach suffers from computational inefficiency, environmental impact, and deployment constraints.
idea.novelty: The paper challenges the dominant scaling paradigm and advocates for a downscaling approach. It proposes a holistic framework to maintain performance while drastically reducing resource demands.
idea.contribution: The paper outlines practical strategies for transitioning away from traditional scaling. It provides a framework for sustainable, efficient, and accessible LLM development.
idea.method: The paper is a position piece without technical details. It advocates for strategies such as model pruning, distillation, efficient architectures, and data curation to achieve downscaling.
idea.detail_reason: Downscaling aims to reduce computational and environmental costs while preserving model utility. The proposed strategies leverage existing techniques to optimize resource usage.
idea.limitation: The paper does not present empirical results or detailed implementation guidelines. The feasibility and performance trade-offs of downscaling remain to be validated.

experiment.process: No experiments conducted; the paper is conceptual.

references: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
The Cost of Training NLP Models: A Scalable Cloud Perspective
Efficient Transformers: A Survey

evolution_steps.from_title: ActiveLLM: Large Language Model-based Active Learning for Textual Few-Shot Scenarios

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's ActiveLLM method relies on querying a large LLM for each instance selection, incurring high computational cost and latency, especially in iterative active learning scenarios.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A advocates for downscaling strategies such as model pruning, knowledge distillation, and efficient architectures to reduce LLM size and resource demands.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B uses a large LLM as a black-box instance selector; A proposes adopting smaller, compressed models but does not specify how to adapt them to active learning pipelines.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A does not provide empirical validation or concrete methods to apply downscaling to active learning with LLMs, leaving B's computational cost limitation unaddressed in practice.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's assumption that LLMs (even smaller ones) are necessary for effective instance selection, without questioning the role of LLMs in active learning.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's ActiveLLM method relies on querying a large LLM for each instance selection, incurring high computational cost and latency, especially in iterative active learning scenarios.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B uses a large LLM as a black-box instance selector; A proposes adopting smaller, compressed models but does not specify how to adapt them to active learning pipelines.

evolution_steps.from_title: Bridging the Gap between Different Vocabularies for LLM Ensemble

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's ensemble method requires multiple large LLMs, incurring high computational cost.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A advocates for downscaling a single LLM via pruning, distillation, and efficient architectures, eliminating the need for ensemble.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B relies on vocabulary alignment for multi-model ensemble; A proposes to avoid ensemble entirely by making a single model efficient.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B's method depends on overlapping tokens for vocabulary mapping; A does not address this because it moves to single-model efficiency.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A still assumes the existence of a base LLM that can be downscaled, inheriting B's assumption that LLMs are the core building block.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's ensemble method requires multiple large LLMs, incurring high computational cost.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B relies on vocabulary alignment for multi-model ensemble; A proposes to avoid ensemble entirely by making a single model efficient.

evolution_steps.from_title: D-CPT Law: Domain-specific Continual Pre-Training Scaling Law for Large Language Models

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's scaling law requires costly initial experiments and assumes fixed relationships that may not generalize to diverse domains or architectures.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A advocates for a paradigm shift to downscaling through pruning, distillation, and efficient architectures, aiming to reduce resource demands.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B provides a quantitative scaling law for upscaling via domain-specific pre-training; A proposes a qualitative framework for downscaling without quantitative predictions.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A does not provide a predictive law or framework to estimate the performance trade-offs of downscaling strategies without extensive empirical testing, inheriting B's reliance on experiment-based tuning.
gap_analysis.what_persists: The assumption that empirical validation is necessary for assessing model performance under new data distributions or architectures.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's scaling law requires costly initial experiments and assumes fixed relationships that may not generalize to diverse domains or architectures.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B provides a quantitative scaling law for upscaling via domain-specific pre-training; A proposes a qualitative framework for downscaling without quantitative predictions.

evolution_steps.from_title: Ensemble Learning for Heterogeneous Large Language Models with Deep Parallel Collaboration

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's ensemble method requires multiple large LLMs, incurring high computational cost and memory usage, making it impractical for resource-constrained settings.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A proposes a paradigm shift towards downscaling, advocating for model pruning, distillation, efficient architectures, and data curation to reduce model size and cost, making LLMs more accessible.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B introduces a novel ensemble aggregation method using relative representation theory and search-based inverse transformation; A provides no technical method but a framework of downscaling strategies as an alternative to scaling.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B's approach of combining heterogeneous LLMs via relative representations remains unaddressed by A's focus on single-model downscaling; no method for ensemble when multiple models are necessary.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A retains the assumption that LLM capability is tied to model size, arguing for reduction but not addressing the scenario where multiple specialized models might be beneficial.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's ensemble method requires multiple large LLMs, incurring high computational cost and memory usage, making it impractical for resource-constrained settings.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B introduces a novel ensemble aggregation method using relative representation theory and search-based inverse transformation; A provides no technical method but a framework of downscaling strategies as an alternative to scaling.

evolution_steps.from_title: Merge, Ensemble, and Cooperate! A Survey on Collaborative Strategies in the Era of Large Language Models

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B surveys collaborative strategies that assume availability of large, resource-heavy models and do not address the sustainability or efficiency of the strategies themselves.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A proposes a paradigm shift away from scaling up, advocating for downscaling via pruning, distillation, efficient architectures, and data curation to make LLMs sustainable and accessible.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A replaces the focus on scaling and collaboration with a focus on reducing model size and resource usage, without providing technical implementation details.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: Neither B nor A presents new experimental validation; the effectiveness and trade-offs of the proposed collaborative or downscaling strategies remain unsubstantiated.
gap_analysis.what_persists: Both papers are conceptual or survey-style, lacking empirical evidence or detailed implementation guidelines to support their claims.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B surveys collaborative strategies that assume availability of large, resource-heavy models and do not address the sustainability or efficiency of the strategies themselves.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A replaces the focus on scaling and collaboration with a focus on reducing model size and resource usage, without providing technical implementation details.

evolution_steps.from_title: Scaling Laws for Predicting Downstream Performance in LLMs

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's method requires training fully-converged sampling models up to 3B parameters, which is still computationally expensive, and assumes a smooth mapping from pre-training loss to downstream performance, limiting applicability to tasks where emergence is not loss-correlated.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A proposes a framework of downscaling strategies (pruning, distillation, efficient architectures, data curation) to reduce computational costs, shifting focus from scaling up to scaling down.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B uses empirical scaling laws (FLOPs→loss→performance) for prediction; A replaces this with a position advocating for resource-efficient development without providing a new predictive mechanism.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B's fundamental need for large compute in training sampling models to derive predictions is not addressed; A's downscaling strategies lack empirical validation and do not offer a cheaper method for performance prediction.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A silently inherits B's assumption that model performance can be characterized by computational budgets (now in reverse for downscaling), without challenging the reliance on extensive training for evaluation.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's method requires training fully-converged sampling models up to 3B parameters, which is still computationally expensive, and assumes a smooth mapping from pre-training loss to downstream performance, limiting applicability to tasks where emergence is not loss-correlated.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B uses empirical scaling laws (FLOPs→loss→performance) for prediction; A replaces this with a position advocating for resource-efficient development without providing a new predictive mechanism.

evolution_steps.from_title: Small Language Models: Survey, Measurements, and Insights

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B surveys existing SLMs but provides no actionable strategy or framework for transitioning from large-scale models to efficient small ones; it catalogs techniques without prescribing how to combine or prioritize them.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A advocates for a paradigm shift from scaling up to downscaling, outlining a high-level framework that bundles strategies like pruning, distillation, efficient architectures, and data curation, but stays at the position level without concrete implementation.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B is a descriptive survey of existing SLMs; A is a prescriptive position piece advocating for downscaling, but neither offers novel mechanisms—A's contribution is a call to action rather than a technical method.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A does not provide empirical validation, implementation guidelines, or quantitative analysis of trade-offs for downscaling strategies, leaving the feasibility and performance impact unaddressed—exactly the gap B had by not analyzing compression or quantization techniques in depth.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's implicit assumption that open-source models and standard evaluation benchmarks are sufficient for assessing downscaling, without questioning whether real-world deployment constraints require different metrics or data.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B surveys existing SLMs but provides no actionable strategy or framework for transitioning from large-scale models to efficient small ones; it catalogs techniques without prescribing how to combine or prioritize them.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B is a descriptive survey of existing SLMs; A is a prescriptive position piece advocating for downscaling, but neither offers novel mechanisms—A's contribution is a call to action rather than a technical method.

evolution_steps.from_title: Scaling Laws with Vocabulary: Larger Models Deserve Larger Vocabularies

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's scaling-law approach prescribes larger vocabularies for larger models without considering the inefficiency and environmental cost of scaling, thereby perpetuating a scaling-centric paradigm.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A proposes a framework of downscaling strategies (pruning, distillation, efficient architectures, data curation) to move away from scaling, but does not provide technical mechanisms or empirical validation.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A shifts the objective from scaling for loss reduction to downscaling for efficiency, whereas B was focused on scaling laws for optimal vocabulary size under compute budget.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B's limitation that its scaling laws were only validated up to 3B parameters and may not generalize to larger models is not addressed by A, which provides no empirical results at any scale.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's assumption that the key metric is final performance (loss or accuracy); A aims to maintain performance while reducing size, similar to B's aim of improving performance via scaling.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's scaling-law approach prescribes larger vocabularies for larger models without considering the inefficiency and environmental cost of scaling, thereby perpetuating a scaling-centric paradigm.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A shifts the objective from scaling for loss reduction to downscaling for efficiency, whereas B was focused on scaling laws for optimal vocabulary size under compute budget.

b94ecba2348841cccb649f36eb81f5f6c98e532b.title: ActiveLLM: Large Language Model-based Active Learning for Textual Few-Shot Scenarios

entities.name: GPT-4
entities.description: Large Language Model used by ActiveLLM for instance selection

entities.name: o1
entities.description: Large Language Model used by ActiveLLM for instance selection

entities.name: Llama 3
entities.description: Large Language Model used by ActiveLLM for instance selection

entities.name: Mistral Large
entities.description: Large Language Model used by ActiveLLM for instance selection

entities.name: BERT
entities.description: Classifier model enhanced by ActiveLLM in few-shot scenarios

entities.name: ADAPET
entities.description: Few-shot learning method compared against ActiveLLM

entities.name: PERFECT
entities.description: Few-shot learning method compared against ActiveLLM

entities.name: SetFit
entities.description: Few-shot learning method compared against ActiveLLM

idea.background: Active learning is designed to minimize annotation efforts by prioritizing informative instances, but many strategies struggle with a cold-start problem requiring substantial initial data. This limitation reduces their utility in few-shot scenarios where instance selection is critical.
idea.novelty: ActiveLLM introduces the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4, o1, Llama 3, and Mistral Large for instance selection in active learning. It overcomes the cold-start problem and outperforms both traditional active learning methods and few-shot learning methods like ADAPET, PERFECT, and SetFit.
idea.contribution: The primary method is ActiveLLM, which leverages LLMs to select informative instances for annotation. Core components include the LLM-based instance selector and a BERT classifier. The approach enhances classification performance in few-shot scenarios and can be extended to non-few-shot settings to help other active learning strategies overcome cold-start problems.
idea.method: ActiveLLM uses a Large Language Model to rank unlabeled instances based on their potential to improve the classifier. The selected instances are then annotated and added to the training set. The BERT classifier is fine-tuned on the accumulated labeled data. The process can be iterated, allowing for sequential selection beyond few-shot settings. Key design choices include the specific LLM used and the prompt design for instance selection.
idea.detail_reason: LLMs possess extensive pre-trained knowledge, enabling them to identify informative instances even with very few initial labeled examples. This makes ActiveLLM effective in cold-start and few-shot scenarios by leveraging semantic understanding to prioritize diverse and challenging instances.
idea.limitation: The method relies on access to LLMs via API or local deployment, which may incur computational costs or latency. The performance may depend on the LLM's domain knowledge and prompt engineering. Additionally, the approach has been validated primarily on text classification; its effectiveness on other tasks or modalities is not yet demonstrated.

experiment.process: Experiments compare ActiveLLM against traditional active learning methods (e.g., uncertainty sampling) and few-shot methods (ADAPET, PERFECT, SetFit) on text classification tasks using BERT classifiers. Performance is measured in terms of accuracy after a limited number of annotations.
experiment.technical_details: ActiveLLM uses GPT-4, o1, Llama 3, or Mistral Large for instance selection. BERT is used as the base classifier, fine-tuned with selected instances. The selection process involves prompting the LLM to score or rank unlabeled instances.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include random sampling, uncertainty-based active learning, and few-shot learning methods ADAPET, PERFECT, and SetFit. ActiveLLM is also compared to itself in non-iterative vs iterative modes.
experiment.verification: Results show that ActiveLLM consistently outperforms baselines in few-shot settings, achieving higher accuracy with fewer annotated examples. Ablation studies demonstrate the benefit of LLM selection over random or heuristic selection.

853340c415719d2125c865356750edc3ad435a43.title: Bridging the Gap between Different Vocabularies for LLM Ensemble

entities.name: EVA
entities.description: Ensemble LLMs via Vocabulary Alignment, a method to bridge lexical gaps among different LLMs for step-wise ensemble.

entities.name: Vocabulary Alignment
entities.description: Technique to map vocabularies of different LLMs into a unified space using overlapping tokens.

idea.background: Previous works on ensembling LLMs are limited to selecting or blending completely generated outputs, which prevents dynamic correction during generation. This is due to vocabulary discrepancies among different LLMs.
idea.novelty: EVA enables fine-grained ensemble at each generation step by learning mappings between vocabularies and projecting output distributions into a unified space, unlike prior work that only ensembles final outputs.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include learning vocabulary mappings via overlapping tokens, projecting LLM output distributions into a unified space for step-wise ensemble, and a filtering strategy to exclude models generating unfaithful tokens.
idea.method: First, mappings between vocabularies of different LLMs are learned with assistance from overlapping tokens. Then, these mappings project output distributions into a unified space, allowing fine-grained ensemble at each generation step. Finally, a filtering strategy discards models that produce unfaithful tokens based on the alignment quality.
idea.detail_reason: Vocabulary alignment leverages overlapping tokens as anchors to create robust mappings, ensuring that distributions from different LLMs are comparable. The filtering strategy maintains ensemble quality by removing unreliable models, preventing contamination from less accurate outputs.
idea.limitation: The method relies on the existence of overlapping tokens between LLM vocabularies; if vocabularies are highly disjoint, mapping may be less effective. Additionally, the filtering strategy assumes that unfaithful tokens can be reliably detected.

experiment.process: EVA was evaluated on commonsense reasoning, arithmetic reasoning, machine translation, and data-to-text generation tasks.
experiment.technical_details: Multiple LLMs were ensembled using vocabulary alignment; distributions were projected and averaged step-wise, with a filtering step based on token faithfulness.
experiment.baseline: Baselines likely include selecting a single LLM output or blending complete sequences, as well as other ensemble methods for LLMs.
experiment.verification: Experiments across diverse tasks demonstrate superiority over baselines, verifying that step-wise ensemble via vocabulary alignment improves performance.

references: Enhancing LLM Performance by Ensembling Multiple Models
Vocabulary Alignment for Cross-Model Transfer Learning
Dynamic Ensemble Methods for Text Generation

199cbde8658dee02643f0352a62ab51657924d08.title: Ensemble Learning for Heterogeneous Large Language Models with Deep Parallel Collaboration

entities.name: DeePEn
entities.description: Training-free ensemble framework for heterogeneous LLMs using probability distribution fusion via relative space mapping and inverse transformation.

entities.name: relative representation theory
entities.description: Theory used to map probability distributions from individual LLM vocabularies to a universal relative space to enable aggregation.

entities.name: search-based inverse transformation
entities.description: Method to convert aggregated distribution from relative space back to the main model's probability space for token selection.

idea.background: Existing LLM ensembling methods require training an extra reward or fusion model to select or combine candidate answers, which hinders generalization to unseen data. Prior approaches also rely on textual responses as communication media, ignoring valuable information in internal representations.
idea.novelty: DeePEn is a training-free ensemble framework that fuses probability distributions from heterogeneous LLMs at each decoding step, overcoming the token misalignment issue by mapping distributions to a universal relative space based on relative representation theory, and using a search-based inverse transformation to produce the next token.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include: (1) mapping each LLM's probability distribution to a universal relative space using relative representation theory, enabling aggregation across different vocabularies; (2) aggregating the mapped distributions; (3) devising a search-based inverse transformation to map the aggregated result back to the main model's probability space for token generation.
idea.method: At each decoding step, each LLM generates a probability distribution over its own vocabulary. DeePEn maps these distributions to a universal relative space using relative representation theory, which aligns tokens from different vocabularies based on their relative positions. The mapped distributions are then aggregated (e.g., averaged). To select the next token, a search-based inverse transformation is applied to convert the aggregated distribution back to the probability space of a chosen main model, enabling token selection from that model's vocabulary.
idea.detail_reason: Mapping to the relative space effectively addresses the vocabulary discrepancy between heterogeneous LLMs, as it captures the relative relationships between tokens rather than relying on exact token matches. The search-based inverse transformation allows the ensemble to operate without additional training, maintaining flexibility and efficiency.
idea.limitation: The abstract does not explicitly list limitations, but potential drawbacks include the computational cost of the search-based inverse transformation and the assumption that relative representation theory generalizes well across diverse LLMs. Additionally, the method may require careful selection of the main model for inverse transformation.

experiment.process: Not described in the provided abstract.

5528acb501a7e4d29971208967be19fada5651e7.title: D-CPT Law: Domain-specific Continual Pre-Training Scaling Law for Large Language Models

entities.name: Continual Pre-Training (CPT)
entities.description: Further pre-training of LLMs on domain-specific data to improve performance in that domain.

entities.name: Scaling Law
entities.description: Empirical relationships that predict model performance as a function of compute, dataset size, or model size.

entities.name: D-CPT Law
entities.description: Proposed scaling law for domain-specific continual pre-training that predicts performance based on mixture ratio, model size, and dataset size.

entities.name: mixture ratio
entities.description: Ratio of general-corpus to domain-corpus used during continual pre-training.

entities.name: general-corpus
entities.description: Large, diverse corpus (e.g., Dolma, Slim-pajama) used for general language understanding.

entities.name: domain-corpus
entities.description: Corpus specific to a downstream domain (e.g., math, code).

idea.background: Continual Pre-Training (CPT) is used to adapt LLMs to specific domains, but choosing the optimal mixture ratio between general and domain corpora is costly, often requiring grid-search with high GPU consumption. Existing methods do not guarantee optimality and are inefficient for varying model sizes.
idea.novelty: This work introduces the D-CPT Law, a scaling law that predicts downstream and general performance for any mixture ratio, model size, and dataset size using only small-scale training experiments. It eliminates the need for expensive grid searches and provides a principled way to select the optimal mixture ratio.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include fitting the D-CPT Law to limited experimental data, enabling performance prediction across mixture ratios, model sizes, and dataset sizes. The law is then used to identify the optimal mixture ratio for a given compute budget. The approach is extended to cross-domain settings, showing broader applicability.
idea.method: The D-CPT Law is formulated as a function of mixture ratio (r), model size (N), and dataset size (D). The functional form is inspired by previous scaling laws, typically involving power-law terms and cross terms. Training is conducted on small-scale models with a few mixture ratios to estimate parameters. The fitted law then predicts performance for larger models and datasets. Key design choices include the specific parametric form and the training budget for fitting.
idea.detail_reason: The chosen scaling law method is effective because prior work (e.g., Kaplan et al., Hoffmann et al.) shows that performance follows predictable power-law trends with model and data size. By incorporating the mixture ratio, the D-CPT Law captures the trade-off between general and domain knowledge. Small-scale experiments are sufficient to fit the law, drastically reducing computational costs.
idea.limitation: The D-CPT Law assumes a fixed relationship that may not hold for extremely diverse domains or unusual mixture ratios. It requires initial experiments to fit the law, and the law's accuracy may degrade for very different model architectures or data distributions. Additionally, the cross-domain extension may not capture complex interactions between multiple domains.

experiment.process: Experiments are conducted on multiple domains (e.g., math, code) using various model sizes (e.g., 125M to 1.3B parameters). A small set of mixture ratios are used for training small models to fit the D-CPT Law. The fitted law is then used to predict performance for unseen ratios and larger models, which is compared against actual results.
experiment.technical_details: Models are based on GPT-2 or LLaMA architectures. Corpora include Dolma (general) and domain-specific datasets (e.g., CodeSearchNet for code, MathQA for math). Training uses standard language modeling loss. The law is fit via least-squares on log-space data.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include grid search over mixture ratios, random search, and the commonly used heuristic of 1:1 ratio. The D-CPT Law is compared in terms of optimal ratio found and computational cost.
experiment.verification: The core idea is verified by showing that the D-CPT Law predictions closely match actual downstream and general performance across various model sizes and ratios. Plots of predicted vs. actual losses and performance metrics demonstrate the law's accuracy. Efficiency is shown by the reduced number of required training runs.

references: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
Continual Pre-Training of Language Models

6013c87aa7d6fa6bbe40764df2a59aaa876cddf8.title: Merge, Ensemble, and Cooperate! A Survey on Collaborative Strategies in the Era of Large Language Models

entities.name: Merging
entities.description: Integrating multiple LLMs in the parameter space to create a single model.

entities.name: Ensemble
entities.description: Combining the outputs of various LLMs to improve prediction.

entities.name: Cooperation
entities.description: Leveraging different LLMs to allow full play to their diverse capabilities for specific tasks.

idea.background: Large Language Models (LLMs) trained on different corpora exhibit varying strengths and weaknesses, posing challenges in maximizing overall efficiency and versatility. Recent studies have explored collaborative strategies to address these challenges.
idea.novelty: This survey provides a comprehensive categorization of collaborative strategies into three primary approaches: Merging, Ensemble, and Cooperation. It offers the first systematic overview of this emerging area, highlighting motivations and potential applications.
idea.contribution: The paper categorizes collaborative strategies into Merging (parameter-space integration), Ensemble (output combination), and Cooperation (task-specific division of labor). It provides in-depth introductions to each method from different perspectives and outlines future research directions.
idea.method: The survey reviews literature on merging (e.g., weight averaging, model fusion), ensemble (e.g., voting, mixture of experts), and cooperation (e.g., routing, delegation). It discusses techniques, trade-offs, and practical deployment considerations based on existing implementations.
idea.detail_reason: The categorization clarifies the landscape of LLM collaboration, helping researchers identify suitable strategies for different goals (e.g., efficiency vs. performance). The survey synthesizes disparate works, offering actionable insights for designing collaborative systems.
idea.limitation: As a survey, it does not present new experimental results. The effectiveness of each strategy heavily depends on model architectures and task characteristics. The coverage may be limited by the fast-evolving nature of the field.

experiment.process: Not applicable (survey paper); the paper reviews existing experimental setups from cited works.
experiment.verification: The paper validates its categorization through comprehensive literature review and discussion of use cases.

references: LLM-BLENDER: A Framework for Ensemble of Large Language Models
Model Merging for Multi-Task Learning in Language Models
Mixture of Experts: A Survey on Collaborative Learning

fa2a637f6532562a9eff1f5e9fef4438aae3f28b.title: Scaling Laws for Predicting Downstream Performance in LLMs

entities.name: FLP
entities.description: Two-stage approach: first estimate function from FLOPs to pre-training loss, then map loss to downstream performance.

entities.name: FLP-M
entities.description: Extension of FLP, details not fully provided in snippet.

entities.name: Pre-training loss
entities.description: Computation-efficient metric for downstream performance estimation.

idea.background: Accurate prediction of downstream performance for LLMs before training is crucial. Prior scaling laws use performance of smaller models to predict target model performance, but emergent abilities at specific computational thresholds pose challenges. Direct FLOPs-to-performance mapping is less accurate.
idea.novelty: The paper introduces using pre-training loss as a more computation-efficient metric instead of directly using FLOPs. It proposes a two-stage approach (FLP) that decouples resource-to-loss and loss-to-performance mappings, enabling accurate prediction with smaller sampling models.
idea.contribution: The primary method is FLP: first estimate a function mapping FLOPs to pre-training loss using fully-converged sampling models, then map pre-training loss to downstream performance using intermediate models with emerged abilities. This avoids training large models for performance estimation.
idea.method: The method has two stages. Stage 1: Train a series of sampling LMs to full convergence and fit a function from FLOPs to pre-training loss. Stage 2: Use intermediate models that have emerged performance to learn a mapping from pre-training loss to downstream task performance. The final prediction combines both stages. Models used: sampling LMs up to 3B parameters to predict 7B and 13B LLMs.
idea.detail_reason: Pre-training loss is chosen because it is cheaper to compute than full downstream performance, and it correlates well with final performance after emergence. The two-stage approach leverages the monotonic relationship between FLOPs and loss and the threshold nature of emergent abilities.
idea.limitation: The approach requires fully-converged sampling models which may still be expensive. It assumes a smooth mapping from loss to performance and may not generalize to tasks where emergence is not well-captured by loss. The paper only validates on up to 13B models.

experiment.process: The authors train a series of small sampling LMs (up to 3B) to full convergence. They fit a function from FLOPs to pre-training loss. Then they use intermediate models with emerged performance to learn a mapping from loss to downstream task accuracy. Finally, they predict performance of 7B and 13B LLMs.
experiment.technical_details: Fully-converged sampling models are trained with different FLOPs budgets. Pre-training loss is measured. Downstream tasks are used for performance evaluation. The mapping functions are likely parametric (e.g., power law).
experiment.baseline: The main baseline is the FLOPs-to-Performance approach, which directly predicts downstream performance from FLOPs. The paper shows FLP outperforms this baseline significantly.
experiment.verification: Experiments verify by comparing predicted vs actual performance on 7B and 13B LLMs, achieving error margins of 5% and 10% respectively, demonstrating the effectiveness of the two-stage approach.

references: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models

9d851cd329dbcb5b4fc6a56c277303cfbd9c646e.title: Small Language Models: Survey, Measurements, and Insights

entities.name: Small Language Models
entities.description: Transformer-based, decoder-only language models with 100M-5B parameters deployed on smart devices.

entities.name: Transformer decoder-only architecture
entities.description: Neural network architecture that autoregressively generates text, commonly used in SLMs.

idea.background: Large language models (LLMs) dominate research, but small language models (SLMs) are widely used in smart devices. However, SLMs have received less academic attention, with few comprehensive surveys on their architectures, training, and deployment trade-offs.
idea.novelty: First comprehensive survey focusing on SLMs with 100M-5B parameters, analyzing architectures, training data, and algorithms, along with on-device benchmark measurements of latency and memory.
idea.contribution: Survey of 70 open-source SLMs; analysis of architectures, training datasets, and algorithms; evaluation on commonsense reasoning, math, in-context learning, and long context; benchmark of inference latency and memory.
idea.method: Selected decoder-only transformer-based SLMs with 100M-5B parameters. Categorized models by architecture innovations (e.g., attention variants, layer composition), training dataset composition (e.g., data sources, filtering), and training algorithm choices (e.g., objectives, learning rate schedules). Evaluated using standard benchmarks (HellaSwag, WinoGrande, GSM8K, etc.) and measured inference latency and memory on a single device with varying sequence lengths.
idea.detail_reason: The survey approach provides a structured overview of the SLM landscape, enabling identification of trends and gaps. Benchmarking on-device runtime offers practical insights for deployment, highlighting trade-offs between model size, capability, and efficiency.
idea.limitation: Survey limited to open-source models; may miss proprietary SLMs. Evaluation benchmarks may not cover all real-world tasks. No in-depth analysis of model compression or quantization techniques.

experiment.process: Evaluated SLMs on multiple benchmarks including commonsense reasoning (HellaSwag, WinoGrande), mathematics (GSM8K), in-context learning, and long context. Also benchmarked inference latency and memory footprint on a single device under controlled settings.
experiment.technical_details: Used standard evaluation frameworks (e.g., lm-eval-harness). For latency, measured per-token generation time using batch size 1. Memory measured via peak GPU memory usage during inference.
experiment.baseline: Compared models against each other across different sizes and architectures; no single fixed baseline but cross-comparison highlights trends.
experiment.verification: Experiments demonstrate trade-offs between model size, capability, and runtime cost, confirming that smaller models can achieve competitive performance on specific tasks while being more efficient.

references: Attention is All You Need
Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

96686b8a1cd281584b1f4812a124a434dbda1c17.title: Scaling Laws with Vocabulary: Larger Models Deserve Larger Vocabularies

entities.name: LLaMA2-70B
entities.description: A large language model with 70 billion parameters, used as a case study for vocabulary size optimization.

entities.name: IsoFLOPs analysis
entities.description: A method for predicting optimal vocabulary size by analyzing models with fixed computational budgets.

entities.name: derivative estimation
entities.description: A technique to estimate the optimal vocabulary size by computing derivatives of the loss with respect to vocabulary size.

entities.name: parametric fit
entities.description: A method to fit a parametric function to the loss as a function of vocabulary size and compute budget.

idea.background: Previous scaling law research focused on model parameters and training data size, largely ignoring vocabulary size. This work extends scaling laws to incorporate vocabulary as a key factor influencing model performance and compute efficiency.
idea.novelty: The main innovation is the systematic investigation of vocabulary size in scaling laws, showing that larger models require larger vocabularies for optimal performance. Three complementary prediction approaches (IsoFLOPs, derivative estimation, parametric fit) are proposed to determine compute-optimal vocabulary size.
idea.contribution: The primary methods include IsoFLOPs analysis, derivative estimation, and parametric fitting of the loss function to predict optimal vocabulary size. These methods converge on the conclusion that vocabulary size should scale with compute budget, leading to the recommendation that Llama2-70B should have used a vocabulary of at least 216K tokens.
idea.method: Models ranging from 33M to 3B parameters were trained on up to 500B characters with various vocabulary sizes. The loss function was analyzed across different FLOPs budgets to derive scaling laws. Three prediction approaches were used: fixing FLOPs and varying vocabulary (IsoFLOPs), computing loss derivatives, and fitting a parametric function. The optimal vocabulary was then determined for given compute budgets.
idea.detail_reason: The methods are effective because they provide multiple independent estimates that converge to the same conclusion, increasing confidence. The parametric fit allows generalization to unseen compute budgets, and validation on 3B-parameter models confirms the predictions empirically.
idea.limitation: The study is limited to models up to 3B parameters and may not fully generalize to larger models. The predictions are based on training on characters, and downstream tasks were only evaluated on a limited set. Additionally, the optimal vocabulary size may depend on the tokenization algorithm used.

experiment.process: Models with 3B parameters were trained across different FLOPs budgets while varying vocabulary size. Performance was measured on downstream tasks to validate the predicted optimal vocabulary.
experiment.technical_details: Training used up to 500B characters with multiple vocabulary configurations. Loss curves were analyzed to compute optimal vocabulary via IsoFLOPs, derivatives, and parametric fitting.
experiment.baseline: Baselines included common vocabulary sizes such as 32K tokens, as used in many LLMs like Llama2-70B.
experiment.verification: Experiments showed that using the predicted optimal vocabulary size (e.g., 43K instead of 32K) consistently improved downstream performance, confirming the core idea that larger models need larger vocabularies.

references: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models
Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models
LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models

frontier_title: Need a Small Specialized Language Model? Plan Early!
trajectory_ascii:
Need a Small Specialized Language M... (2025)
├── The case for 4-bit precision: k-bit... (2022)  (background)
├── SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient... (2022)  (background)
├── DeepSpeed- Inference: Enabling Effi... (2022)  (background)
├── Data Selection for Language Models ... (2023)
├── Distilling Step-by-Step! Outperform... (2023)  (background)
└── Sheared LLaMA: Accelerating Languag... (2023)  (background)
trajectory_description: The main research lines in this tree focus on improving the efficiency of large language models through quantization (4-bit precision, SmoothQuant), optimized inference (DeepSpeed-Inference), data selection via importance resampling, knowledge distillation, and structured pruning (Sheared LLaMA). These lines converge in the frontier paper, "Need a Small Specialized Language Model? Plan Early! (2025)," which argues that instead of post-hoc compression, deliberately planning a small model from the start—by integrating insights from these efficient training and compression methods—yields better performance and resource use. Overall, the evolution story moves from optimizing existing large models for deployment to a proactive design philosophy where efficiency is built into the model architecture and training process from the beginning.

1a7103d169cede62cc7d579c7addf12d18c11668.title: Need a Small Specialized Language Model? Plan Early!

entities.name: Importance sampling
entities.description: Resampling the pretraining set to imitate specialization data for training a small model.

entities.name: Projected networks (PN)
entities.description: A novel architecture where a large network's parameters are linearly projected into a small network for specialization.

idea.background: Large language models are versatile but inefficient for small inference budgets. Small models have efficient inference but require specialization to achieve good performance. Prior work often focuses on either pretraining from scratch or fine-tuning, but not on targeted data selection or parameter projection for specialization.
idea.novelty: The paper introduces two novel solutions: importance sampling for resampling the pretraining set when retraining from scratch, and projected networks (PN) for cheaply adapting a single pretrained model. These methods leverage limited specialized data effectively, unlike standard fine-tuning which may not align with small model capacity constraints.
idea.contribution: The primary methods are importance sampling to select relevant pretraining data for a small model, and projected networks where a large model's parameters are linearly projected into a small specialized model. These enable obtaining good specialized small models with limited specialized data.
idea.method: In scenario 1, importance sampling is used to resample the generic pretraining set to match the distribution of the specialized data, then a small model is trained on the resampled data. In scenario 2, a large 'projected network' is trained with a linear projection layer that maps its parameters to a small network, which is then used for specialization. The projection is learned jointly with the large network.
idea.detail_reason: Importance sampling is effective because it biases the pretraining distribution towards the target domain without requiring additional labeled data. Projected networks are effective because they allow the large model to learn a representation that can be compressed linearly, retaining relevant information while reducing inference cost.
idea.limitation: The methods assume the availability of a large generic pretraining set and a limited amount of specialized data. They may not perform well if the specialization data is extremely scarce or if the domain is very different from the pretraining distribution. The projection-based method requires training the large network from scratch for each new base model.

experiment.process: Experiments are conducted across various domains, training set sizes, and training budgets. Both scenarios are evaluated separately, comparing the proposed methods against baselines like standard fine-tuning and training on specialized data alone.
experiment.technical_details: Small models are trained with the same architecture as the large model but with fewer parameters. Importance sampling uses a kernel density estimate to weight pretraining examples. Projected networks use a linear projection matrix that is trained end-to-end.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include training a small model only on specialized data, fine-tuning a large pretrained model, and training a small model on the full pretraining set.
experiment.verification: The core idea is verified by showing that the proposed methods outperform baselines in terms of accuracy per inference cost, especially when specialized data is limited.

evolution_steps.from_title: The case for 4-bit precision: k-bit Inference Scaling Laws

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B only evaluates zero-shot performance on quantized models, lacking a method to specialize a model for a specific domain with limited data.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A uses importance sampling to select relevant pretraining data for a small model and projected networks to transfer knowledge from a large model, enabling specialization with limited data.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A shifts from quantization for memory efficiency to data selection and parameter projection for specialization.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B's limitation on the poor scaling of sophisticated quantization methods beyond simple baselines remains unaddressed; A does not improve quantization techniques.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A still relies on the assumption that a large generic pretraining set is available, similar to B's reliance on large models for scaling laws.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B only evaluates zero-shot performance on quantized models, lacking a method to specialize a model for a specific domain with limited data.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A shifts from quantization for memory efficiency to data selection and parameter projection for specialization.

evolution_steps.from_title: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's data selection via importance resampling relies on hashed n-gram features and a small set of target samples to define the target distribution, but it does not address how to obtain a small specialized model with limited specialized data.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A introduces two methods: (1) importance sampling to select generic pretraining data that matches the distribution of limited specialized data, then training a small model; (2) projected networks where a large model's parameters are linearly projected into a small network, learned jointly during training.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A extends B's importance resampling to the scenario of training small specialized models and adds a projection-based method for model compression, while B only focuses on data selection for general pretraining.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A's importance sampling method inherits B's reliance on a small set of target samples to define the target distribution; if those samples are unrepresentative, the selected data may be suboptimal for the specialized domain.
gap_analysis.what_persists: The assumption that a small set of unlabeled domain-specific samples can adequately characterize the target distribution for data selection.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's data selection via importance resampling relies on hashed n-gram features and a small set of target samples to define the target distribution, but it does not address how to obtain a small specialized model with limited specialized data.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A extends B's importance resampling to the scenario of training small specialized models and adds a projection-based method for model compression, while B only focuses on data selection for general pretraining.

evolution_steps.from_title: Distilling Step-by-Step! Outperforming Larger Language Models with Less Training Data and Smaller Model Sizes

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's multi-task framework requires LLM-generated rationales as additional supervision, which can be noisy, expensive to obtain, and may not be useful for all domains or languages.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A avoids rationales by using importance sampling to select pretraining data that matches the specialized domain, and by using projected networks to linearly transfer parameters from a large model to a small model without needing explicit rationales.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A replaces rationale-based multi-task learning with data selection and parameter projection, removing the dependency on LLM-generated rationales.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: B's method assumes availability of a powerful LLM with useful rationales; A similarly assumes availability of a large generic pretraining set or the ability to train a large network, inheriting the dependency on extensive pre-existing resources.
gap_analysis.what_persists: Both methods require a large-scale external resource (LLM or large pretraining set) to enable effective transfer to the small specialized model, and neither addresses scenarios where such resources are unavailable or costly.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's multi-task framework requires LLM-generated rationales as additional supervision, which can be noisy, expensive to obtain, and may not be useful for all domains or languages.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A replaces rationale-based multi-task learning with data selection and parameter projection, removing the dependency on LLM-generated rationales.

evolution_steps.from_title: Sheared LLaMA: Accelerating Language Model Pre-training via Structured Pruning

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's structured pruning only compresses a generic pre-trained model without tailoring the pretraining data distribution to the target specialized task, and it requires a pre-trained large model.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A uses importance sampling to select generic pretraining data that matches the specialized data distribution, and optionally learns a linear projection from a large model's parameters to a small network, enabling effective specialization without post-hoc compression.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B prunes a pre-trained large model to a smaller size using structured removal of layers and heads, while A either trains a small model from scratch on importance-sampled data or jointly trains a small model via a learned projection during large model training.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A's methods still assume the availability of a large generic pretraining set and may fail when specialized data is extremely scarce or the target domain is far from the pretraining distribution; also, the projection method requires training a large network from scratch for each new base model.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits the assumption from B that a large generic pretraining set is available, and both require significant computational resources to handle large models.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's structured pruning only compresses a generic pre-trained model without tailoring the pretraining data distribution to the target specialized task, and it requires a pre-trained large model.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B prunes a pre-trained large model to a smaller size using structured removal of layers and heads, while A either trains a small model from scratch on importance-sampled data or jointly trains a small model via a learned projection during large model training.

evolution_steps.from_title: SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's quantization method requires a pre-trained large model and may introduce accuracy degradation, especially at low bit-widths or with outlier-heavy activations.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A avoids quantization error by training a small specialized model from scratch using importance sampling for data selection and a linear projection layer to transfer knowledge from a large model.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: B compresses an existing large model via post-training quantization; A constructs a new small model via data curation and parameter projection from a large model.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A's projection method requires training a large model from scratch for each new base model, inheriting B's assumption that a large model is available, and does not address scenarios where only a quantized model exists.
gap_analysis.what_persists: Both methods assume the existence of a large generic model or pretraining set; A does not challenge B's reliance on a large model.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's quantization method requires a pre-trained large model and may introduce accuracy degradation, especially at low bit-widths or with outlier-heavy activations.

what_changed.mechanism_change: B compresses an existing large model via post-training quantization; A constructs a new small model via data curation and parameter projection from a large model.

evolution_steps.from_title: DeepSpeed- Inference: Enabling Efficient Inference of Transformer Models at Unprecedented Scale

evolution_trace.parent_problem: B's DeepSpeed-Inference system efficiently deploys large transformer models but assumes that large models are necessary; it does not provide a method to obtain small specialized models that perform well without the resource cost of large models.
evolution_trace.child_solution: A introduces importance sampling to select relevant pretraining data from a large generic set and projected networks that linearly map a large model's parameters into a small specialized model, enabling effective small models without large-scale training.
evolution_trace.mechanism_delta: A adds data curation via importance sampling and parameter projection to transfer knowledge from large to small models, whereas B only focuses on efficient inference of given large models without altering their size or training data.

gap_analysis.remaining_gap: A still requires availability of a large generic pretraining set or a large pretrained model for projection; it does not address inference efficiency of the resulting small models on edge devices or extremely low-resource settings, which B had as an open problem.
gap_analysis.what_persists: A inherits B's assumption that large models or large pretraining corpora are accessible, without exploring scenarios where only limited data and compute are available.

evolution_steps.trigger_gap: B's DeepSpeed-Inference system efficiently deploys large transformer models but assumes that large models are necessary; it does not provide a method to obtain small specialized models that perform well without the resource cost of large models.

what_changed.mechanism_change: A adds data curation via importance sampling and parameter projection to transfer knowledge from large to small models, whereas B only focuses on efficient inference of given large models without altering their size or training data.

unresolved_gaps.gap_text: A's importance sampling method inherits B's reliance on a small set of target samples to define the target distribution; if those samples are unrepresentative, the selected data may be suboptimal for the specialized domain.
unresolved_gaps.ancestor_assumption: The assumption that a small set of unlabeled domain-specific samples can adequately characterize the target distribution for data selection.

e61fde1309a9f5aab2060ace6f709711823c9ca5.prune_reason: Studies emergent abilities via loss, not about constructing small specialized models.

d03a9b2a0e090cc9fd2ba0a457ecea35372f1018.title: Demystifying Prompts in Language Models via Perplexity Estimation
