53535d38fe259a3aa7c911edd8048d764e09e8e1.title: The case for 4-bit precision: k-bit Inference Scaling Laws

entities.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
entities.description: Models like BLOOM, OPT, NeoX/Pythia, GPT-2 studied for zero-shot performance under quantization.

entities.name: Quantization
entities.description: Method to reduce bit-precision of model parameters, trading accuracy for memory and latency.

entities.name: Inference Scaling Laws
entities.description: Empirical study of how zero-shot performance scales with model size and bit-precision.

idea.background: Previous quantization methods reduce bit-width to compress LLMs, but the trade-off between model size and accuracy at different precision levels is not well understood. Prior work focused on specific quantization techniques without systematic scaling analysis across multiple model families and sizes.
idea.novelty: This work develops inference scaling laws for zero-shot performance across 3-8 bit precision and model sizes from 19M to 176B parameters, running over 35,000 experiments. It identifies that only small block size and quantization data type (Int vs Float) improve the bit-level scaling trade-off, while other methods fail.
idea.contribution: The primary contribution is a large-scale empirical study establishing scaling laws for k-bit quantization. Core components include quantizing model parameters (k-bit) while keeping inputs 16-bit, and testing across multiple LLM families. The function is to determine the optimal bit-precision and model size for maximizing zero-shot accuracy given a fixed memory budget.
idea.method: Experiments use 16-bit inputs and k-bit parameters (3-8 bits) for models from 19M to 176B parameters. Quantization is applied to full-precision weights using various methods. Key design choices include varying block size for independent quantization groups and using integer vs floating-point data types. The study covers BLOOM, OPT, NeoX/Pythia, and GPT-2 families.
idea.detail_reason: Small block size reduces quantization errors by grouping parameters into fine-grained blocks, while the data type (Int vs Float) affects the representation range and precision. These factors are shown to be the only significant improvements in scaling behavior, highlighting the difficulty of surpassing the naive scaling trade-off.
idea.limitation: The study finds it challenging to improve the bit-level scaling trade-off beyond the simple baselines, suggesting that more sophisticated quantization methods may not help. It assumes fixed 16-bit inputs and only evaluates zero-shot performance, not fine-tuned or few-shot settings. The experiments are limited to the four model families and specific quantization configurations.

experiment.process: Over 35,000 experiments were conducted by varying model size (19M to 176B), bit-precision (3-8 bits), quantization method, block size, and data type, measuring zero-shot accuracy on standard benchmarks.
experiment.technical_details: Models were loaded with 16-bit inputs and k-bit quantized parameters. Quantization used block-wise scaling factors and either integer or floating-point representations. Implementations likely leverage PyTorch and custom kernels.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include full-precision models and naive quantization without block splitting or type selection. Comparative methods vary quantization granularity and data type.
experiment.verification: The scaling laws are verified by plotting zero-shot accuracy vs. total bit count (model size × bits) and showing that only block size and data type create noticeable deviations from the baseline trend.

references: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
Quantization and Training of Neural Networks for Efficient Integer-Arithmetic-Only Inference
The case for 4-bit precision: k-bit Inference Scaling Laws

74013b7cfa0fc524803350fca51341004565eb22.title: Data Selection for Language Models via Importance Resampling

entities.name: DSIR
entities.description: Data Selection with Importance Resampling, a framework for selecting pretraining data for language models.

entities.name: Importance resampling
entities.description: Statistical method adapted for high-dimensional text data selection by estimating weights in a reduced feature space.

entities.name: Hashed n-gram features
entities.description: Efficient feature representation used to estimate importance weights in DSIR, enabling scalability.

entities.name: Pile dataset
entities.description: Large-scale dataset used in experiments to demonstrate DSIR's efficiency (100M documents in 4.5 hours).

entities.name: KL reduction
entities.description: Metric defined to measure proximity between selected pretraining data and target distribution on features.

idea.background: Selecting a suitable pretraining dataset is crucial for general-domain and domain-specific language models. Existing methods rely on simple heuristics or manual curation by human experts, which are insufficient for large-scale raw text data. The paper formalizes this as selecting a subset to match a target distribution given unlabeled target samples.
idea.novelty: The paper extends classic importance resampling, originally used in low dimensions, to high-dimensional text data for language model pretraining data selection. It proposes a reduced feature space via hashed n-gram features to make importance weight estimation tractable, replacing heuristic or manual curation with a principled statistical approach.
idea.contribution: DSIR efficiently selects data by estimating importance weights in a reduced feature space (hashed n-grams) and then resampling according to those weights. The framework includes a metric (KL reduction) to assess how well selected data matches the target distribution. It can select 100M documents from the full Pile dataset in 4.5 hours.
idea.method: DSIR uses hashed n-gram features to reduce the dimensionality of raw text data. Importance weights are computed for each document in the raw dataset based on the ratio of target to raw feature densities. Data is then selected via importance resampling, where documents are sampled with probability proportional to these weights. The hashing trick enables efficient processing of large vocabularies. The target distribution is derived from a small set of unlabeled target samples.
idea.detail_reason: The chosen methods are effective because hashed n-gram features preserve relevant aspects of the data for language modeling while enabling efficient computation. Importance resampling provides a principled way to reweight the raw data distribution to match the target, and the KL reduction metric empirically validates that the selected data indeed aligns with the target in feature space.
idea.limitation: The abstract is truncated, but potential limitations include reliance on target samples that may not fully represent the desired distribution, and the assumption that hashed n-gram features capture all relevant data characteristics. The method may also require tuning of the hash size and n-gram order for different domains.

experiment.process: The paper applies DSIR to select subsets from the Pile dataset to match a target distribution. It measures KL reduction to quantify alignment. Efficiency is demonstrated by processing 100M documents in 4.5 hours.
experiment.technical_details: Hashed n-gram features are used with a specific hash size (e.g., 2^21) and n-gram order (e.g., 3). Implementation in Python with efficient data pipelines. Experiments run on a single machine with multiple CPUs.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include random selection, heuristic-based selection (e.g., perplexity filtering), and manual curation. Comparisons likely show DSIR achieves lower KL divergence or better downstream performance.
experiment.verification: Experiments verify that importance resampling with hashed n-gram features produces data that better matches the target distribution (lower KL reduction) than heuristics, while being computationally efficient.

references: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners (GPT-3 paper)
Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code (Codex paper)
The Pile: An 800GB Dataset of Diverse Text for Language Modeling

aad167be3c902388ea625da4117fcae4325b8b7d.title: Distilling Step-by-Step! Outperforming Larger Language Models with Less Training Data and Smaller Model Sizes

entities.name: Distilling step-by-step
entities.description: A new mechanism that trains smaller models to outperform LLMs using less training data by incorporating LLM rationales as additional supervision in a multi-task framework.

entities.name: LLM
entities.description: Large language models like PaLM, which are memory inefficient and compute-intensive for practical applications.

entities.name: Small task-specific model
entities.description: Smaller models trained via finetuning or distillation; this paper shows they can outperform LLMs with Distilling step-by-step.

entities.name: Rationale
entities.description: Intermediate reasoning steps or explanations extracted from LLMs and used as additional supervision during training.

idea.background: Deploying large language models (LLMs) is challenging due to memory inefficiency and high computational cost. Researchers train smaller task-specific models via finetuning with human labels or distillation using LLM-generated labels, but both methods require large amounts of training data to match LLM performance.
idea.novelty: This paper introduces Distilling step-by-step, which trains smaller models to outperform LLMs while using less training data than standard finetuning or distillation. The key innovation is leveraging LLM rationales as additional supervision in a multi-task framework, enabling the small model to learn from the reasoning process rather than just the final output.
idea.contribution: The primary method is Distilling step-by-step, which extracts rationales from LLMs and trains a smaller model in a multi-task framework to predict both the final answer and the rationale. This approach allows the small model to achieve better performance than both finetuning and distillation with fewer labeled/unlabeled examples, and to outperform few-shot prompted LLMs with substantially smaller model sizes.
idea.method: The method uses a multi-task training framework where a small model (e.g., T5) is trained to simultaneously predict the task label and generate the rationale extracted from an LLM. The training procedure involves supervised learning on both the label and the rationale, with the rationale acting as additional supervision. Key design choices include extracting rationales from a large LLM like PaLM and using a sequence-to-sequence architecture like T5 to handle both label and rationale generation.
idea.detail_reason: The method is effective because rationales provide intermediate reasoning steps that capture the LLM's decision-making process, allowing the smaller model to learn richer supervision beyond the final label. This reduces the need for large amounts of training data and enables the small model to generalize better, even outperforming the LLM in certain cases.
idea.limitation: The method relies on access to an LLM to generate rationales, which can be computationally expensive and may introduce noise if the rationales are not accurate. Additionally, the approach assumes that the LLM can produce useful rationales for the task, which may not hold for all domains or languages.

experiment.process: Experiments were conducted on 4 NLP benchmarks. The method (Distilling step-by-step) was compared to standard finetuning, distillation, and few-shot prompting of large LLMs. Performance was measured in terms of accuracy and data efficiency.
experiment.technical_details: The small model used was a 770M parameter T5 model, and the large LLM was a 540B parameter PaLM model. Rationales were extracted from PaLM via few-shot prompting. The multi-task framework trained T5 to output both the label and the rationale.
experiment.baseline: Baselines included finetuning the small model with human labels, distillation using LLM-generated labels without rationales, and few-shot prompted PaLM (540B) and other LLMs.
experiment.verification: The core idea was verified by showing that the 770M T5 model trained with Distilling step-by-step outperformed the few-shot prompted 540B PaLM model, and achieved better performance than finetuning and distillation with fewer training examples.

references: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer
PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways
Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

2c994fadbb84fb960d8306ee138dbeef41a5b323.title: SmoothQuant: Accurate and Efficient Post-Training Quantization for Large Language Models

entities.name: SmoothQuant
entities.description: Training-free, accuracy-preserving post-training quantization solution for LLMs, enabling W8A8 quantization.

entities.name: Large language models (LLMs)
entities.description: Models like OPT, BLOOM, GLM, MT-NLG, Llama-1/2, Falcon, Mistral, Mixtral that are compute and memory intensive.

entities.name: Post-training quantization (PTQ)
entities.description: Quantization method applied after training, without retraining.

entities.name: INT8 quantization
entities.description: 8-bit integer quantization of weights and activations for matrix multiplications.

entities.name: Activation outliers
entities.description: Challenging activation values that make quantization difficult; smoothed by migrating difficulty to weights.

idea.background: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate excellent performance but are compute- and memory-intensive. Quantization can reduce memory and accelerate inference, but existing methods cannot simultaneously maintain accuracy and hardware efficiency. SmoothQuant addresses this by enabling 8-bit weight, 8-bit activation quantization without accuracy loss.
idea.novelty: Existing PTQ methods for LLMs fail to balance accuracy and hardware efficiency. SmoothQuant is the first training-free, general-purpose solution that preserves accuracy while enabling W8A8 quantization across diverse LLMs. It introduces a mathematically equivalent transformation to smooth activation outliers, migrating quantization difficulty from activations to weights.
idea.contribution: SmoothQuant enables INT8 quantization for both weights and activations in all matrix multiplications of LLMs, including OPT, BLOOM, GLM, MT-NLG, Llama-1/2, Falcon, Mistral, and Mixtral. It achieves up to 1.56x speedup and 2x memory reduction with negligible accuracy loss, allowing a 530B model to run on a single node. The code is publicly available.
idea.method: SmoothQuant uses an offline, mathematically equivalent transformation that smooths activation outliers by scaling activations and correspondingly scaling weights. This migration of quantization difficulty from activations to weights makes both easy to quantize to INT8. The method is applied to all linear layers in the transformer architecture. No retraining or calibration data is required beyond a small set for scaling factor computation.
idea.detail_reason: Weights are naturally easy to quantize due to their uniform distribution, while activations have outlier values that hurt quantization. By smoothing these outliers via a per-channel scaling, the transformed activations become more amenable to quantization, and the scaled weights remain quantizable. This transformation is reversible at inference, ensuring mathematical equivalence.
idea.limitation: SmoothQuant is a post-training quantization method and may not achieve optimal accuracy for all model sizes or bit-widths below 8-bit. It assumes the availability of a small calibration dataset for computing scaling factors. The method focuses on linear layers and may not address quantization challenges in other components like embeddings or normalization.

experiment.process: SmoothQuant was evaluated on a range of LLMs including OPT, BLOOM, GLM, MT-NLG, Llama-1/2, Falcon, Mistral, and Mixtral. Measurements of throughput (speedup) and memory consumption were taken, and accuracy was assessed on standard downstream tasks (e.g., perplexity, zero-shot accuracy).
experiment.technical_details: Quantization was applied to all GEMM operations using INT8. Scaling factors were computed from a small calibration set (e.g., 128 samples). Inference was performed using PyTorch and custom CUDA kernels for INT8 matrix multiplication.
experiment.baseline: Compared against FP16 and INT8 baselines from prior PTQ methods such as LLM.int8() and ZeroQuant. Also compared against the original FP16 model without quantization.
experiment.verification: Accuracy was shown to be within 0.1% of the FP16 baseline for most models, while achieving up to 1.56x speedup and 2x memory reduction. The ability to serve a 530B model on a single node demonstrated practical scalability.

references: LLM.int8(): 8-bit Matrix Multiplication for Transformers at Scale
ZeroQuant: Efficient and Affordable Post-Training Quantization for Large-Scale Transformers
GPTQ: Accurate Post-Training Quantization for Generative Pre-trained Transformers

abdb0f9d1486dbb024c4bc9f8f9dc40464c58715.title: Sheared LLaMA: Accelerating Language Model Pre-training via Structured Pruning

entities.name: Sheared-LLaMA
entities.description: A series of smaller LLMs pruned from LLaMA2-7B to 1.3B and 2.7B parameters using structured pruning.

entities.name: LLaMA2-7B
entities.description: The base large language model with 7 billion parameters that is pruned to create Sheared-LLaMA.

entities.name: Targeted structured pruning
entities.description: A pruning method that removes entire layers, heads, and intermediate/hidden dimensions to achieve a specified target shape end-to-end.

entities.name: Dynamic batch loading
entities.description: A technique that updates the composition of sampled data in each training batch based on varying losses across different domains.

entities.name: Pythia
entities.description: A suite of open-source LLMs used as baseline for comparison.

entities.name: INCITE
entities.description: An open-source LLM used as baseline for comparison.

entities.name: OpenLLaMA
entities.description: An open-source reproduction of LLaMA used as baseline.

entities.name: TinyLlama
entities.description: A concurrent open-source small LLM used as baseline.

idea.background: The popularity of LLaMA and other moderate-sized LLMs has highlighted the potential of building smaller yet powerful LLMs, but training such models from scratch on trillions of tokens remains costly. Previous work includes training smaller models from scratch, which requires substantial compute.
idea.novelty: The main innovations are using structured pruning to derive smaller LLMs from a larger pre-trained model, with targeted pruning to a specific shape, and dynamic batch loading to handle domain imbalance during training. This approach significantly reduces compute compared to training from scratch.
idea.contribution: The primary methods are (1) targeted structured pruning that removes layers, heads, and hidden dimensions end-to-end to reach a target shape; (2) dynamic batch loading that adjusts data composition based on domain losses. These enable pruning LLaMA2-7B to 1.3B and 2.7B parameters, outperforming state-of-the-art models of equivalent size with only 3% of the compute.
idea.method: The architecture is derived from LLaMA2-7B by pruning entire layers, attention heads, and intermediate/hidden dimensions in an end-to-end manner to match a target shape (e.g., 1.3B or 2.7B parameters). The training procedure uses dynamic batch loading, where the composition of sampled data in each training batch is dynamically updated based on the varying losses across different domains. Key design choices include end-to-end pruning without layer-wise fine-tuning and adaptive data sampling to focus on underperforming domains.
idea.detail_reason: Targeted structured pruning is effective because it leverages the pre-trained knowledge of the larger model while reducing parameter count to a specific shape, avoiding the need for full retraining. Dynamic batch loading addresses the issue of domain imbalance by focusing on high-loss domains, improving training efficiency and final performance.
idea.limitation: The method requires a pre-trained large model, so it cannot be applied to training from scratch. Additionally, the pruned models, while competitive, may still have capacity limitations compared to the original large model, and the approach may not generalize to all model families or architectures.

experiment.process: The process involves pruning LLaMA2-7B to target sizes of 1.3B and 2.7B parameters using targeted structured pruning and dynamic batch loading, followed by evaluation on a wide range of downstream tasks and instruction tuning evaluations.
experiment.technical_details: Pruning is performed end-to-end by removing entire layers, attention heads, and intermediate/hidden dimensions. Dynamic batch loading updates the composition of data per batch based on domain losses, implemented via a sampler that adjusts probabilities.
experiment.baseline: Baselines include state-of-the-art open-source models of equivalent sizes: Pythia, INCITE, OpenLLaMA, and the concurrent TinyLlama models.
experiment.verification: The experiments verify that Sheared-LLaMA outperforms these baselines on downstream and instruction tuning evaluations while requiring only 3% of the compute compared to training a similar-sized model from scratch.

references: LLaMA: Open and Efficient Foundation Language Models
LLaMA 2: Open Foundation and Fine-Tuned Chat Models
The Pythia Scaling Suite: Analysis of Scaling and Interpreting Large Language Models

c022f75b00d795c6297d6a9ea948856ea4d365a1.title: DeepSpeed- Inference: Enabling Efficient Inference of Transformer Models at Unprecedented Scale

entities.name: DeepSpeed-Inference
entities.description: A multi-GPU and heterogeneous inference system for transformer models.

entities.name: Multi-GPU inference
entities.description: Solution for dense and sparse transformers that fit in aggregate GPU memory, minimizing latency and maximizing throughput.

entities.name: Heterogeneous inference
entities.description: Solution leveraging CPU/NVMe/GPU memory for high-throughput inference of models larger than aggregate GPU memory.

idea.background: Transformer model inference faces challenges due to growing diversity in model size, characteristics, and latency/throughput requirements. Existing inference systems often lack versatility to handle such variety efficiently.
idea.novelty: DeepSpeed-Inference introduces a multi-GPU inference solution that minimizes latency and maximizes throughput for both dense and sparse transformers, and a heterogeneous inference solution that leverages CPU/NVMe/GPU memory for models larger than aggregate GPU memory, enabling unprecedented scale.
idea.contribution: The paper presents (1) a multi-GPU inference solution to minimize latency while maximizing throughput for dense and sparse transformers when the model fits in aggregate GPU memory, and (2) a heterogeneous inference solution that leverages CPU/NVMe/GPU memory to enable high-throughput inference for models larger than aggregate GPU memory.
idea.method: The system supports both dense and sparse transformer models. For multi-GPU inference, it employs efficient parallelism and communication patterns. For heterogeneous inference, it combines CPU, NVMe, and GPU memory, minimizing data movement and overlapping computation with I/O. Key design choices include dynamic partitioning and intelligent caching.
idea.detail_reason: The methods are effective because they reduce latency by 6.4x and increase throughput by 1.5x over state-of-the-art, achieving over 50% peak performance on A6000 GPUs. The heterogeneous approach allows inference on models 25x larger than GPU-only solutions, enabling trillion parameter scale inference.
idea.limitation: The system assumes models can be partitioned across GPUs or stored in CPU/NVMe memory, which may require significant engineering effort. It may not be optimized for very low-latency single-GPU inference. The paper does not address inference for extremely small models or edge devices.

experiment.process: The system was evaluated on various transformer models, measuring latency and throughput across different model sizes and hardware configurations.
experiment.technical_details: Used multiple NVIDIA A6000 GPUs, compared to state-of-the-art inference systems with similar hardware.
experiment.baseline: State-of-the-art inference solutions (e.g., NVIDIA TensorRT, FasterTransformer).
experiment.verification: Results show 6.4x latency reduction and 1.5x throughput improvement, along with 84 TFLOPS performance, verifying the core innovations in multi-GPU and heterogeneous inference.

references: DeepSpeed: System Optimizations Enable Training Deep Learning Models with Over 100 Billion Parameters
Megatron-LM: Training Multi-Billion Parameter Language Models Using Model Parallelism
GPipe: Efficient Training of Giant Neural Networks using Pipeline Parallelism
